title: Bryan Taylor: There Is No Equity Risk Premium (Investing in America Series) #635

segments.text: Unfortunately, current government policy is not following the twig theory.
I mean, they're following restrictions on trade through tariffs.
We're currently involved with a war in Iran.
Inflation is picking up.
Stop markets provided double digit returns in the 1920s, 50s, 80s, the 2010s.
And if you follow that logic, the 2040s are going to be providing great returns.
On the other hand, that does not bode well for the 2030s.
There was this period, almost 70 years.
Essentially, the majority of the 20th century, where US bonds had a negative real return.
I mean, that's absolutely true.
Welcome to a special series of the mep favor show on the past, present and future of America.
I'm sitting down with some of the most notable historians, thinkers, investors,
and US financial history, all tied to my new coffee table book, Investing in America.
The rise of a 250 year bull market out to live for us.
Metfabers, the co-founder and chief investment officer at Cambria Investment Management.
Did it industry regulations?
He will not discuss any of Cambria's funds on this podcast.
All opinions expressed by podcast participants are solely their own opinions
and do not reflect the opinion of Cambria Investment Management or its affiliates.
For more information, visit Cambriainvestments.com.
This episode is brought to you by Ivy Invest.
You know, I've talked a lot on the show about endowment style investing, the Yale model,
the Swinson Playbook, public markets, private equity, private credit, real estate,
all blended together into a portfolio built for the long game.
The data on this approach is pretty compelling.
The problem has always been the same.
It was basically impossible for regular investors to actually do it.
Well, here's somebody who lived through that world from the inside.
Wendy Lee spent 18 years managing multi-billion dollar endowment funds in New York.
And at some point, she had a pretty simple realization.
This is exactly how she had won investor own money, but even she couldn't access it as an individual.
So she built a thing that didn't exist.
Ivy Invest is one of the first endowment style funds designed for everyday investors.
You get a single fund with exposure to private equity, real estate, private credit,
and public markets, the whole mix starting at just a thousand bucks.
I actually had Wendy on the podcast go back and listen to that episode.
It's worth your time.
For now, check them out at ivinvest.co.
That's ivyinvest.co.
Before investing, carefully consider the investment objective, risk,
charges, and expenses of the institutional investment strategy fund.
The prospectus contains this and other information.
Pass performance does not guarantee future results.
Learn more at ivyinvest.co.
Welcome back, everybody.
It's summertime.
Today we got a special episode, our returning guests.
I think it's four time is Dr. Brian Taylor, founder, chief economist of global financial data,
also known as Finneon, Brian and his team have built the most comprehensive database
of historical financial market data in the world.
He just published a new book, five financial errors, how financial markets transform the world,
which uses nine centuries stock bond, build data, explain what actually drives returns
across history.
Today we're focusing the lens a little bit on the US, but we'll look all around the world.
Brian, welcome back to the show.
Oh, thank you very much.
You talk about a couple hundred years of returns.
You know, most people only focus on the last, first of all, they only focus on the last
17, but really if they focus maybe the last 20 of their own lifetime,
talk to us about, you have some pretty controversial takes in this book.
What's the most important thing to you at two hundred and what 30,
50 years of US stock bond tell us, what are your main takes from this twig framework?
Okay.
Well, twig is an acronym standing for taxes, war, inflation and the government.
And if you look back at returns over time,
if those factors are in the right combination,
you get the highest returns to the stock markets for investors.
Well, excuse me, for trade, T is for trade.
And you want free trade because the countries that have been successful over time
in providing high returns to investors are the countries that have pursued trade.
I mean, if you look at the countries like Switzerland or Singapore,
which have a lot of exports to the rest of the world, they have done very well.
Countries that have used import substitution that have tried to avoid trade
such as Brazil or Argentina have done poorly for investors.
The W stands for war.
War is bad for investors, period.
For several reasons, one, there's the destruction that goes along with it.
Second, the government intervenes to control the environment,
reducing the amount of investment that goes into the private sector.
The government raises taxes.
The government uses inflation as a way of funding the war.
All of those are negative outcomes for investors.
And if you look back in the past,
in the periods when a country like Austria or Germany had very poor returns
or Japan, it was during the war because of the costs of conducting the war.
The eye stands for inflation.
Inflation is the worst enemy affixed income investors
because once inflation goes up, that's money that's lost forever.
You can't get it back.
And so if you look at the hyperinflations that occurred in Germany,
in Japan after World War II and so forth, it just totally wiped out investors.
And if you look globally at the returns between 1965 and 1981,
that affected everyone.
Everyone got low returns throughout the developed world because of the inflation.
The G is for government intervention.
If the government is intervening in the economy, either through nationalization
or completely taking over the economy,
then that's going to provide poor returns to investors.
So what I have learned from my analysis of the past is that
if all of these four factors are coordinated correctly,
if there is a maximum of trade, no war,
minimum of inflation and a minimum of government intervention,
that's when investors get the highest returns possible.
However, if you have the opposite, which is restrictions on trade,
war, inflation, government intervention,
that's when returns are the lowest.
That's when they can be negative and investors actually end up losing money.
So that was my takeaway from my several centuries of analysis of financial markets.
And, you know, unfortunately, the government doesn't always look at these factors
and try to follow those rules to maximize returns to investors.
But if they want to focus on that, that's how they can maximize the returns.
Try to put this in context a little bit for people.
If you look at kind of US history, which has got a couple of hundred years here,
is there been a time where you think that like all four of these have been firing
in the right direction where like, all right, like everything is looking good.
Things are working on the twig theory.
And then also we can flip that and say, is there been a time when all four are looking not so good?
Well, I think the 1980s and the 1990s are a very good example of when all the
pistons were firing correctly because you were promoting free trade.
You had a change from the canteen era to the Reagan era.
You had no war.
You had the peace dividend back there.
You were reducing inflation.
You had disinflation.
And then the under Reagan Bush, the government was trying to reduce its role in the economy.
And consequently, you had double digit returns for two decades in the United States.
On the other hand, during World War One, you had just the opposite.
You had war, you had inflation, you had the government intervening in the economy.
You had restrictions on trade because of the war.
And consequently, the 1910s are one of the few decades in which there were negative returns to investors.
So you can take those two examples and just see how the twig theory works.
If we were to look at it today, we got to grade it in 2026 of the four, what's the biggest tailwind
and what's the biggest headwind?
They all look good.
All look bad.
Is it a mixed bag?
Well, unfortunately, current government policy is not following the twig theory.
I mean, they're following restrictions on trade through tariffs.
We're currently involved with a war in Iran.
Inflation is picking up.
The government is trying to promote AI.
So that's a positive.
So, you know, what I would see is that you need to reverse some of those policies,
not use the tariffs to restrict trade, but to promote trade to every degree possible.
I mean, obviously in the war, as far as possible,
and I think doing that will reduce the level of inflation in the economy.
So that would be my recommendations.
So much of us managing money today, particularly the older cohort, you base all your decisions
on kind of learned history, what you experience.
And there was this magical, we were kind of spoiled 1980s, 1990s period.
And even on end of the 2000s for bonds specifically, but you talk about in the book,
you're like, you know, hey, look, there was this period, almost 70 years.
So essentially, the majority of the 20th century were US bonds from their early teens to the 1981,
where they had a negative real return.
Yes.
I don't know how many people know that.
I mean, that's absolutely true.
In the book, I talk about the two interest rate pyramids, because you had the first interest rate
period pyramid, what happens there is you have an increase in interest rates because of inflation.
And then the government reduces the inflation, which then reduces the interest rates.
The first interest rate pyramid occurred from around 1896 until 1920, mainly driven by World War I
and the inflation that came out of that.
Then you had a decline in interest rates into World War II.
But then the real interest rate pyramid occurred from 1941 until 1981, in which interest rates
went from around 2% on government bonds up to double digit levels, 15, 16%, and then you had
a 40-year period from 1981 to 2021, in which interest rates went from 15% on the 10-year bond
down to under 1%, under a half percent.
And then in Europe, interest rates were negative on the 10-year bond, as well as in Japan.
Now, during 2022, interest rates jumped back up, which imposed losses on all fixed income investors.
And hopefully, the interest rates will stay around the 4% level for some years to come.
I think the Fed is definitely wary of increasing interest rates.
I think Warsh will do a good job on that, but we have to avoid another interest rate pyramid.
And I think they will do that. We shall see.
In the book, you say, look, you want to project out what, in your kind of 20 lessons,
you want to project out what bonds are going to do as a pretty simple predictor.
You want to tell us what it is?
Well, the current yield on the 10-year bond tells you basically what return you're going to get,
because if interest rates go up, you suffer a capital loss.
If interest rates go down, you suffer a capital loss. If interest rates go up, you get a capital gain.
And the two offset each other. And so that way, if interest rates go up from 4% to 10%,
you're going to lose money on your bond investment. And that'll offset the change in the interest rates.
So it is a very good predictor. And of course, with cash, the yield on the treasury bills,
that's going to be primarily dependent upon inflation.
Let's talk about the equity risk premium. You want to get into your thoughts here?
Well, the equity risk premium varies dramatically over time and between countries.
What I did was I looked at the 10-year equity risk premium for each country to see how the
returns to stocks versus bonds changed over time. And part of the discussion of the equity
risk premium is that there is a equity risk premium that exists in the market. And the evidence
shows it simply doesn't exist because the return on bonds and the return on stocks move
independently of one another. And so, for example, in 2021, when you had the large change in interest
rates, that is going to dramatically affect the return to bonds and increase the equity risk premium,
simply because the return to stocks doesn't change, then you're still going to get a high equity
risk premium. You had a high equity risk premium after World War II, simply because
the returns to stocks were increasing. And then the return to bonds was gradually decreasing
because of the fact that you had rising interest rates. And so you have to look at the interplay
of the two. And you can't really say that there is a equity risk premium. What you have to do is
look at where you are at the current situation and then try to predict how the returns to equities
and to bonds going to occur independently of one another over the investment time frame that
you're looking at. Do you know anyone else that believes this? Because I feel like this is one of
those finance tropes of year and it's taught in every school. I don't know if I know anyone else
that believes this. Well, I think that's just because most people haven't done the historical
research to do the analysis across countries. I mean, if you go to the Finneon Guide, which is
available on the SSRN, you have all of this analysis there for 27 different countries over the course
of two centuries. And I don't think anyone has ever done that specific analysis in order to come
to those conclusions. But I think if they review the data that I have collected over the past three
decades, hopefully they will agree with me and learn from it. I think the biggest surprise for people.
So they assume there's equity risk premium. And I think that there's no more iron cloud law in
all of investing that people believe that stocks outperform bonds. And then I ask them, do you
know how long stocks have gone underperforming bonds before? And even in the US, it's decades,
right? And it just feels like a huge amish mass on people what they want to believe is true.
And what is actually true? And I think you've helped eliminate more of that than anybody.
The United States has had a higher return to equities relative to bonds in most countries.
I mean, in many countries, you've had many 10-year periods when equities underperform bonds
is especially true in Europe. And so you have to ask, what is your time horizon? Now certainly,
if you're looking at 10 or 20 years, equities are probably going to outperform bonds. I mean,
that's just the balance of the evidence there. However, if you're looking at a shorter time period,
two or three years, bonds could easily outperform equities or equities underperform. I mean,
that's just what the evidence is. And when I put together all this data in the books,
what I wanted to do was just to make it available so everyone can make their own choices about how to
invest and what to invest in. And what for them is the proper allocation between stocks and bonds.
I mean, when I was a broker, we used to say invest until you can sleep at night. If you can't
sleep at night, then you don't have the proper investment portfolio for yourself. But that depends
upon everybody's individual tolerance for risk. I was listening to a few different videos and
debates and discussions among my kind of favorite market historians. It's like you and McCory and
Arnaut and a bunch of different people, Seagull, Chat and about historical market returns. And
all sorts of different ideas. And it's really fun to think about the history as like a being an
archaeologist. And we'll put those links in the show notes listeners in addition to Brian has some
really great papers on the SSRN, including his most recent Phineon Guide to Global Markets 2026 edition,
which just dropped a couple of months ago that is a must read. But as you read these, again,
it's kind of like you can come to your own conclusions and you look at the data and hey look,
you don't want to believe it great. But you kind of see all these periods and start to understand
that these belief systems that you have may not hold up at a period at which you want them to,
which is an entire career in many cases like 10, 20 years. Like most of us think about yourself in 20
years and you're like, that's two decades for this ironclad belief I have to not be true.
That's hard for people to say Santa's not real, you know.
Well, in another aspect that I bring out in the five financial eras book is the 30-year cycle
in stocks that you've during the past century had 30-year cycles in which given the decade that
you're in, stop markets will do well or do poorly. Stop markets provided double-digit returns
in the 1920s, the 1950s, the 1980s, the 2010s. And you know, if you follow that logic, the 2040s
are going to be providing great returns to investors. On the other hand, you've had poor returns
in the 1940s, the 1970s, the 2000s when you had two vicious bear markets and that does not
bode well for the 2030s. Now, whether that 30-year pattern continues or not, we'll find out
the hard way, but that's just something to be cautious about when you're making your investment
decisions. This is a chart of it's very simple. It's just US stock dividend, the government bond
yield. And if you would, if you would follow it from 1800 to like the 1960s, US dividend yields were
always higher than government bonds. And then they weren't. And then they weren't ever again
almost. Well, up until the 1950s investors looked at the dividend yield and the
interest rate on bonds. That was their primary directive for deciding to invest. Capital gains were
of a secondary importance. But starting after World War II, you had larger capital gains.
And then you had taxes. I mean, you did not have taxes in the 1800s and that affects your decisions
because if you have your money in the stock market, you can delay paying taxes until you sell.
That with no taxes in the 1800s, that provided a different perspective on things. And so these
changes in government policy affect the results in the marketplace. And that shows it.
You guys have a discussion where you're talking about debt and thinking about just general
size of the debt relative to various kind of benchmarks. How do you think about this in the framing
of history, not just in the US, but globally as well? Is that something we should get hot and
bothered about or not worry about so much? In the book I look at both the level of government debt
and also the stock market capitalization. And what I've generally found is that if the stock market
capitalization is greater than the level of government debt within the country, that generally is
positive for stocks. However, if the government debt is greater than the stock market capitalization,
that's generally negative for stocks. Now, what you find in the US is that until the 1980s,
the two sort of moved inversely to one another. And this was true in the UK and in the US
that when the stock market capitalization would increase, the level of government debt would go down.
And I see that as a natural cause and effect in the past because the decline in the government
debt relative to GDP released capital, which could be invested in the stock market was invested
in the stock market and helped the stock market to provide higher returns. Since around 1980, however,
both government debt and stock market capitalization have increased. Now, so far, the stock market
capitalization has increased more than the government debt, which is a positive sign. However,
if that trend were to reverse, that would definitely be a negative for the market. And so you
have to look at the two of them together. You can't look at one in isolation relative to the other.
But it is a matter of concern, because right now in the United States, the government debt is
greater than GDP. And that was fine when interest rates were low, you know, back in the teens,
but now that interest rates are higher, it could become a matter of concern.
I love seeing charts that I've never seen before. I've never seen US dividends as a percentage of
GDP. So talking about some of these GDP figures, and that's interesting too, because US
dividends are pretty low in the US right now. But more than anything that shows the market's growing,
the size of the US stock market is expansive. And the reason I put that in the book is that everyone
talked about, oh, the dividend yield has gone down. And then that's bad for the stock market. But they
don't look at one of the reasons why the dividend yield has gone down is that the stock market
capitalization has increased dramatically. So you can't continue to have a 6% dividend yield
when the stock market capitalization is twice GDP, which is what it currently is. I mean, the two
are going to move inversely to one another. However, if you take that into account the size of
the stock market capitalization and the dividend yield, there really hasn't been that much change
over time. And I think that's an important point which a lot of investors have missed.
So US is somewhere around, you can correct me, I don't know, two thirds, 60% a world market cap
right now and maybe a quarter of GDP. What was the record for US as a percentage of market cap?
Did it hit 70 in the early part of the 20th century? Yeah. During the 1950s, it was definitely 70%.
And then at that point, Canada was 10%. I mean, I talk about the Anglo countries that
throughout history, the Anglo countries, which are the UK, Canada, the United States, Australia,
New Zealand have consistently controlled 70 to 80% of the total stock market capitalization of
the world. And that continues to be true. I mean, I can remember 20 years ago, people are talking
about, oh, emerging markets are going to expand dramatically. You don't want to invest in any of
the developed markets because they're going to shrink. And that just hasn't proved true. I mean,
what's happened is because of the computers, the internet, AI, all of those have been captured
by the United States and expanded dramatically. I mean, you have something like Facebook, which is
throughout every country in the world. And so consequently, the United States has had a higher
return to investors over the past 15 years than emerging markets. And so the United States
constantly comes back. I mean, there are time periods when the US has done poorly relative
to the rest of the world. But then the United States finds a new way to grow its capital markets,
grow its companies, and look at the AI revolution, which we're having right now. I mean,
that's centered here in the United States. Now China also is focusing on growing the AI. But AI is
in the United States. It's not in Europe or other countries. And we'll see 10 years from now exactly
where AI has gone. No one knows where it's going. But it's definitely the current revolution in the
stock market. And all of the large companies are pursuing it for that particular reason.
I mean, there's a lot of technology changes that are occurring. I mean, if you look at the wars in
Ukraine and Iran, drones are driving those wars. Drones weren't used in previous wars. And so you
have a lot of technological revolution in war, in computers, in the internet, everywhere in biotechnology.
I mean, look at how quickly we got a vaccine for COVID when it came out. It was within one year.
That never could have happened in the past. And so technology is driving the stock market. It's
driving the future. And who knows where we'll be 20 years from now. I mean, I constantly say if,
you know, some kid from today was thrown back into the 1970s and didn't have the internet,
didn't have their own phone, didn't have all the things they have today. They'd go bonkers.
What's it going to be like 20 years from now? Nobody knows.
And that's the fun part. You know, reading back through your book, you go back obviously to the
1800s in the US, but hey, back to the 1600s and kind of the founding of the joint stock companies.
And another lens, Amsterdam, and then later London. And you know, it's a similar situation like
imagine talking to some of these people. And I did a tweet recently because we were joking about
South Korea's stock market going kind of baddie the past year. And at least on most measures,
it's now larger than the UK. And I was like, imagine telling someone in the 50s or post-Korean war,
that South Korea would one day have a bigger stock market in our lifetime than the UK. And people
be like, you're crazy. And same thing. Do you think New York and the US has a monopoly on the
kind of foreseeable future as the HQ or do you think history will cause that to shift again in our
lifetime? No, I think as long as the government encourages that, it will continue to be the center
of financial markets for the world. But the government has to be there to help it to achieve that.
You know, and the other thing you didn't point out is that currently Korean per capita GDP is
greater than Japanese per capita GDP. Now a lot of that has been driven by the interest in
semi-conductors that have been produced in Korea. But I mean, that's just how the world constantly
changes. And it will continue to change unpredictable. The cool part about your book is that,
you know, it demonstrates in all these various countries, particularly in the stock market,
but also in bonds and other areas, particularly when you, you, the global markets,
they've sustained and have had great returns over time. And that may, even in the US,
that may have been banks and insurance in the early days, 100 years ago, what railroads,
utilities, and today, tech AI, who knows what else in the coming years? And that's kind of the
beauty of this free market capitalism, I think. What do you think?
No, I agree. I mean, one thing that I've thought about recently is, you know, during the 17th century,
you had the scientific revolution. You had people like Leibniz, Bacon, Galileo, Newton,
bringing forth ideas about nature and analysis of nature and not depending upon
the ancient Greeks for their ideas. The scientific revolution of the 17th century led to the
industrial revolution of the 18th century, in which the steam engines, the railroads and so forth
came forth. And that led to what I call the equity revolution of the 18th hundreds of the 19th
century, because if you look at the GDP, the equity as a market cap as a share of GDP,
at in 1800, it was only 1% by World War II. It was up to 40%. And so every country in the world
by 1914, when World War I began, had their own stock markets, you know, had hundreds of firms
throughout the world, thousands of firms, in which people could invest. I mean, the stock market
completely transformed the world in the 1800s. Angus Madison has done research on GDP. And he
said, you know, if you go from Roman times up until around 1820, there was very small changes in
per capita GDP, but 1820 was sort of a break point. That's when per capita GDP started to grow
for the whole world. And that was precisely when people started creating companies and investing
in equities and driving the revolution that increased the global per capita stock market capitalization
of GDP from 1% up to 40%. Now, the unfortunate thing was that it stayed around 40% from 1914 until
1981. And that's when you had a globalization beginning and you had a second jump from around 40%
up to 100% and 200% now in the United States. And so that's what's really driven the remarkable
growth that's occurred during the past 50 years. People love to talk today about some of these
like mega companies, Apple, Microsoft, and Vidya by the time this hits the publish button, SpaceX
is likely public. How do you think about this in terms of history? Is there some precedent? I
imagine if we go back to the VOC days, there was only a few stocks to even talk about. So they
were pretty concentrated. But has this been the norm or is this something that is particularly
unusual? Well, right now, the concentration in the stock market where you look at the top 10 companies
as a share of the total stock market capitalization is the highest it's ever been. Now, there was a
period back in the 1950s and 1960s when it was also as high as it was today. I think the concern
is that because of the concentration that it's a sign of a bubble and the stock market's going to
collapse as a result. But if you look back at the concentration that occurred in the 50s and 60s,
it didn't necessarily lead to a bubble because then after you had the stock market increasing for
those mega companies back in the 50s and 60s, it spread out to the rest of the market and enabled
the rest of the market to continue to grow. So I don't see it as a negative in the way that some
people say it. I just see it as a sign of the times of what's going on. I think it will continue to
bring growth to the market. Let's hear some of your favorite stats. Are there any particular
things that stick out about financial history, about global history with financial markets that you
think just like you keep coming back to where you're like, man, that's just an interesting tidbit.
That's an interesting story, something that you think most people either may not be familiar with
have never heard before about markets. Well, again, if you go to the five financial eras,
just talking about the changes in the mindset. I mean, the reason I wrote the book was when you
had looked at, when other people had looked at the equity risk premium and tried to analyze
what the equity risk premium was over time, they looked at it from an overall perspective.
Okay, what was the equity risk premium in the United States from 1900 till the present and didn't
really break down the different eras. And when I did my financial archaeology studying the past,
I found that you can't really look at the overall returns over long periods of time. You have to
look at particular periods and see what were the factors that were either encouraging higher returns
or preventing higher returns from encouraging. And that's where I came up with the twig theory.
And so if you look at the world wars and the code war period, that's when markets did
horribly because the government intervened and really prevented the private sector from growing.
But then after 1981, you did have much higher growth because there is more interest in
the private markets. Now that needs to continue in order for the stock markets to continue to grow.
But that's what I would mainly focus on is how the returns have changed over different periods
of time and how the mindsets for investors have changed according to the exogenous events
that happened at each point in time. This summer, Fandoo is the best place to bet on goals,
including equalizers. Yep, headers. Every goal is worth more on Fandoo. So let there be goals.

21 plus in presence in select states. First online real money wager only.
Minimum $5 wager required for seven consecutive days. $5 first deposit required.
Bonus issue does not withdraw but bonus bets which expire seven days after receipt.
Restrictions apply. See full terms at Fandoo.com slash sportsbook gambling problem called 1-800-Gamber
or 1-800-My-Reset. There's a comment that I think it was McCory said in one of his papers where he
talks about the right hand chart bias. And this is a really interesting philosophical topic where
basically exactly what you're talking about. You can divvy up periods where various markets are
what's going on the world. But a lot of people love to just look at the entire history or at that point
in time. And depending on when you clip the right side of the chart that point in time could lead
you to totally different conclusions. And so like so a good example we talk about even on the equity
risk premium back in COVID and comparing US stocks to the long bond because interest rates were
like zero. I was like US stocks and long bonds have had the same returns for like 40 years. And if
you just extrapolated that you're like well I just should never you know US stocks but then of course
the opposite flipped right after. And then you make the decision in 10 years 20 years later you're like
oh my god well US stocks always outperform. Just like depending on where you draw the line in the
sand it changes the whole thing. But if you go by decade or century or whatever period it becomes
quite a bit more apparent that it's a huge variability on outcomes. Right and between 1981
and 2020 you had declining yields on government bonds which allowed bonds to provide higher returns
to all fixed income investors. Well that's not going to happen in the future. I mean interest rate
to jumped up to 4% on the 10 year bond in that range 3% in Europe. And they're not going back
down. So you can't rely upon that in the future. You have to completely change your perspective
and anyone who's just going to look at well what happened since 1981 they're going to get the wrong
perspective. You have to look forward and try to apply how are things going to be in the next 10
years regardless of what happened in the previous 10 years. One of the things that I think you know
everyone's always so scared about bear markets. And if you look back in history in your book
in others you find times there's plenty of bear markets. I think a feature they've been around forever
they happen all the time. Sometimes there's even some big ones. Sometimes there's even some
really big ones. But it feels like you know they always recover particularly on a global basis in
US. However there's been a couple times when they don't where it's a straight zeroing out.
